name: CCTV & IP Video Surveillance Deployment Models
approvers: Blair Henry (Director, IT Operations and Platform Support); Quintin Grant (Sr Manager, Enterprise Architecture)
strategicIntent: Establish a standardized set of deployment models for IP-based video surveillance systems across McCain Foods manufacturing and corporate sites, balancing physical security requirements with network risk exposure. The pattern enforces air-gapped or firewall-controlled topologies to prevent unauthorized access to surveillance infrastructure. All models ensure compliance with McCain's CCTV Surveillance Policy and applicable local regulations.
problemStatement: McCain Foods operates video surveillance systems across 35+ manufacturing and corporate sites without a consistent deployment architecture, creating variable risk exposure and uncontrolled integration with the corporate network. Unmanaged CCTV systems connected to corporate infrastructure without proper segmentation represent a significant attack surface. Additionally, cloud-based storage of surveillance footage is prohibited, requiring standardized local storage and access control models. A clear taxonomy of supported deployment patterns is needed to guide site-level implementation decisions.

useCases.use: New CCTV or IP video surveillance system deployments at manufacturing sites, warehouses, or corporate facilities requiring a standardized, policy-compliant architecture.
useCases.dontUse: Cloud-connected video surveillance solutions or any deployment requiring footage to be stored outside of the local site environment.

useCases.use: Sites requiring remote or corporate network access to video management systems (VMS), where a dedicated VLAN and firewall-controlled integration is mandated.
useCases.dontUse: Wireless camera deployments where a wired solution is feasible, or deployments without a maintenance agreement covering firmware updates, patching, and break/fix support.

useCases.use: Environments where surveillance footage must remain on-premises and access is limited to authorized personnel via VPN or dedicated corporate network segments.
useCases.dontUse: Temporary or ad-hoc surveillance deployments that do not have a designated local vendor, maintenance contract, or site assessment completed prior to installation.

components: IP Cameras — Wired IP-based surveillance cameras deployed at site locations; wireless cameras are discouraged.
Video Management System (VMS) — On-premises software platform for recording, managing, and viewing camera feeds; must be locally hosted with no cloud connectivity.
Dedicated Video Surveillance VLAN — Isolated Layer 2 network segment used to contain all CCTV traffic; required for Model 2 and Model 3 deployments.
Network Video Recorder (NVR) / Storage — Local storage infrastructure for retaining all captured footage; cloud storage is explicitly prohibited.
Firewall (Model 3 only) — Controls ingress and egress traffic between the Corporate network and the dedicated Video Surveillance VLAN; sizing determined by access requirements.
Corporate Network Backbone — McCain's enterprise WAN/LAN infrastructure used as the transport layer in Model 2 and Model 3 deployments.
VPN Gateway — Provides authorized remote access to the VMS in Model 3 deployments; subject to McCain Remote Access Standards.
Network Uplinks — Physical or logical connections between the surveillance VLAN and corporate backbone; must be sized to support requisite video bandwidth.
interactionSteps: Step 1 — Site Assessment: Conduct an on-site assessment to determine physical layout, camera placement, access requirements, and applicable local regulations before selecting a deployment model.

Step 2 — Deployment Model Selection: Select one of three approved models based on access needs — Model 1 (fully standalone, no external access), Model 2 (standalone using corporate backbone, no IP gateway), or Model 3 (corporate network/VPN integrated with firewall).
Step 3 — VLAN Design: For Model 2 and Model 3, design and provision a dedicated Video Surveillance VLAN to isolate all CCTV traffic from general corporate network segments.

Step 4 — Firewall Provisioning (Model 3 only): Size and place a firewall at the boundary between the corporate network and the surveillance VLAN; define access control rules based on documented access requirements.

Step 5 — Model 3 Sub-Model Selection: For Model 3 deployments, determine whether a standalone physical network (Model 3A) or the corporate backbone (Model 3B) will be used for IP video transmission to the VMS.

Step 6 — Bandwidth Planning: Validate that network uplinks between the surveillance environment and the corporate backbone can support the required video transmission bandwidth for the number and resolution of cameras deployed.

Step 7 — Vendor Engagement: Engage a local vendor capable of installing and supporting the full solution end-to-end, including hardware, VMS software, firmware/security patching, and break/fix under a formal maintenance agreement.
Step 8 — Local Storage Configuration: Configure NVR or equivalent local storage to retain footage on-premises; validate that no footage is transmitted to or stored in any cloud environment.
Step 9 — Policy Compliance Validation: Review deployment against McCain's CCTV Surveillance Policy and applicable local jurisdiction laws and regulations before go-live.
Step 10 — Ongoing Maintenance: Ensure the vendor maintenance agreement is active and covers regular firmware updates, security patching, and component replacement for the life of the deployment.
securityConsiderations: All surveillance footage must be stored locally; cloud storage of any CCTV footage is strictly prohibited.
Model 3 deployments must use a dedicated VLAN and a firewall to control all traffic flows between the corporate network and the surveillance environment.
Wired camera installations are strongly preferred over wireless to reduce the risk of signal interception or interference.
All VMS and camera firmware must be kept current via a vendor-managed maintenance agreement that includes regular security patching.
VPN remote access to surveillance systems must comply with McCain's Remote Access Standard and be authenticated through corporate identity controls.
Model 1 (fully standalone) is the preferred deployment from a risk minimization perspective; external access should only be introduced where there is a clear operational need.
Local regulations and privacy laws governing CCTV usage must be reviewed and adhered to prior to deployment at each site.
The VMS and all surveillance infrastructure must not have an active IP gateway to external networks in Model 2 deployments, ensuring no unintended external routing exists.
Physical access to NVR and VMS hardware must be restricted to authorized personnel to prevent tampering or unauthorized footage extraction.
Vendors selected must be well-established and capable of demonstrating a credible long-term support posture for the full solution lifecycle.

guardrails.rule: All CCTV footage must be stored on-premises at the site of capture. Cloud storage of video surveillance data is prohibited.
Any CCTV system connected to the corporate network must reside in a dedicated Video Surveillance VLAN and must not share a VLAN with general IT workloads.
Model 3 deployments must be protected by a firewall that enforces access control between the corporate network and the surveillance VLAN. No direct uncontrolled routing is permitted.
Wired camera infrastructure is the recommended standard. Wireless camera deployments require documented justification and exception approval.
All deployments must have an active maintenance agreement with the local installation vendor covering firmware updates, security patching, break/fix, and hardware replacement.
All deployments must comply with the McCain CCTV Surveillance Policy and applicable local jurisdiction laws before go-live. A site assessment is mandatory.
Only the three approved deployment models (Model 1, Model 2, Model 3A/3B) are supported. Any deviation requires formal exception approval through the SOE standards process.
Network uplinks supporting video transmission must be sized and validated to support the requisite bandwidth prior to deployment to prevent congestion on shared corporate links.
Remote access to VMS systems must be implemented via McCain-approved VPN in accordance with the Remote Access Standard Operating Environment document.

AI-integrated CCTV capabilities are identified as a future investment area (Fiscal 27–28 roadmap). Any AI-enhanced surveillance deployment must be reviewed and approved through the SOE process before adoption.

exceptionProcess: Exceptions to the approved deployment models must be reviewed by the SOE Owner (Kyle Sullivan) in consultation with relevant IS staff including SMEs, Infrastructure Architecture, and Support. The updated exception rationale is documented and circulated to designated approvers (Blair Henry and Quintin Grant) for formal sign-off before any non-standard deployment proceeds. All exceptions are tracked against the SOE document version history.

revisionHistory.author: AI Assistant
revisionHistory.summary: Initial APL entry drafted from McCain CCTV SOE document version 1.2 (December 12, 2025). Captures three deployment models, network segmentation requirements, local storage mandate, and vendor management guardrails.
